original_abstract:
Total suspended particulate (TSP) samples were collected at the summit of Mount Tai (1534 m a.s.l.) in the North China Plain to investigate the impact of regional biomass burning on the free troposphere. Sampling was conducted between 29 May and 28 June 2006, capturing both the peak wheat straw burning season and the subsequent post-burning period. Using capillary GC and GC-MS following water extraction and butyl ester derivatization, we analyzed the samples for low-molecular-weight dicarboxylic acids, ketoacids, and α-dicarbonyls.

Dicarboxylic acids (C2–C11) were the most abundant species identified, with concentrations ranging from 220 to 6070 ng m⁻³. Oxalic acid (C2) was the dominant species (105–3,920 ng m⁻³), followed by succinic (C4) or malonic (C3) acid. We also identified unsaturated aliphatic diacids (maleic, isomaleic, and fumaric) and aromatic diacids (phthalic, isophthalic, and terephthalic). ω-Oxocarboxylic acids (C2–C9) represented the second most abundant class, led by glyoxylic acid, while pyruvic acid and α-dicarbonyls (glyoxal and methylglyoxal) were also present in significant quantities. Notably, these peak concentrations are several times higher than those typically reported at ground level in Chinese megacities.

Temporal analysis revealed a sharp increase in organic acid levels from late May to early June, peaking on 7 June before declining significantly between 8 and 11 June as wind directions shifted from southerly to northerly. These trends mirrored those observed for total carbon (TC) and water-soluble organic carbon (WSOC). The high loading of water-soluble organics is attributed to a combination of direct emissions from wheat straw fires and the subsequent atmospheric processing of precursor gases. This includes photochemical oxidation, dark ozonolysis, accretion reactions, and the degradation of nonvolatile precursors like unsaturated fatty acids. Our findings underscore the profound impact that early summer agricultural fires have on the chemical composition and air quality of the free troposphere over the North China Plain.
humanized_abstract:
To study the influence of regional biomass burning on free troposphere, Total suspended particulate (TSP) samples were collected at the summit of Mount Tai (1534 m a.s.l.) in the North China Plain. The sampling period was from 29 May to 28 June 2006, covering the peak wheat straw burning period and the period after the burning. The dicarboxylic acids, ketoacids and α-dicarbonyls in the samples were determined by capillary GC and GC-MS after extraction with water and derivatization with butyl ester.

Both unsaturated and aromatic diacids were also identified. The dicarboxylic acids (C2–C11) were the most abundant species with concentrations in the range of 220–6070 ng m⁻³. Oxalic acid (C2) was the most abundant species with concentration ranging from 105–3,920 ng m⁻³, sometimes accompanied with succinic or malonic acid. ω-Oxocarboxylic acids (C2–C9) were the second most abundant class with dominant glyoxylic acid. Other species including pyruvic acid and α-dicarbonyls (glyoxal and methylglyoxal) were also highly abundant. These peak concentrations are several times higher than those reported at ground level in Chinese megacities.

We investigate the temporal evolution and spatial distribution of organic acids, total carbon (TC), and water-soluble organic carbon (WSOC) during a free tropospheric episode over the North China Plain (NCP) in which severe smoke layers originating from early wheat straw burning were uplifted into the free troposphere. Temporally resolved mass spectrometric analyses reveal a clear enhancement of organic acids from late May to early June, culminating on 7 June and being strongly decreased between 8 and 11 June, associated with a switch in prevailing wind direction from the south to the north and being closely linked to TC and WSOC. The high loading of WSOC is inferred to result from the wheat straw fire emissions themselves as well as the photochemical and nighttime dark ozonolysis processes and accretion reactions and the volatility changes (oxidation) of non-volatile precursors like unsaturated fatty acids. Overall, this study provides compelling evidence of the dramatic influence of early summer agricultural fires on the free tropospheric composition and air quality over the NCP.
